content:
Target codebase (hourly) has 45+ oRPC contracts, 100+ Prisma models, 50+ React pages, existing Playwright + playwright-bdd setup with auth/login.feature (13 step defs). Rich dataset for MVP validation." 2>&1 && \
DECISION: Match algorithm is tiered (Cucumber expressions → FTS5 fuzzy → LLM-assisted creation) and serves as a GROWTH mechanism. When no Action matches, the algorithm packages graph context as a suggestion for the agent to create a new Action. The graph learns from every feature." 2>&1 && \
DECISION: Scanner architecture is pluggable from day 1. Scanner interface with registry, concrete implementations for oRPC, Prisma, React Router. Extensible to Express, GraphQL, etc." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: MCP server uses @modelcontextprotocol/server with stdio transport, Zod v4 for input schemas, better-sqlite3 sync API for instant responses. All logging to stderr." 2>&1 && \
FACT: CLI uses Commander.js + @commander-js/extra-typings for type-safe args. Binary names: abacus, ab. All commands support --json flag." 2>&1

Switched from TypeScript to Go for core. Reasons: better compiler feedback for agents, single binary distribution, matches beads ecosystem (also Go/cobra), no CGo needed (modernc.org/sqlite). Scanners remain Node.js (BYOASTP)." 2>&1 && \
BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) architecture. Scanners are standalone executables communicating via Scanner Protocol v1 (JSON over stdin/stdout). Abacus core has zero language-specific parsing. Any language can write a scanner." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: Go MCP SDK uses mcp.AddTool() with struct-based input schemas (auto-generated via jsonschema tags). StdioTransport for Claude Code. MCP server is a cobra subcommand in the same binary: abacus mcp-server." 2>&1 && \
FACT: Official Go libraries exist for all needed components: MCP SDK (v1.4.0), cucumber-expressions-go, gherkin/go/v38, modernc.org/sqlite. Zero third-party gaps." 2>&1

TypeScript compiler messages are poor for agent feedback loops. When Sonnet agents hit TS compiler errors, messages are often cryptic ('Type X is not assignable to Y' with deeply nested generics) and lead to thrashing. Go compiler messages are precise, actionable, and include suggestions ('did you mean X?'). For agent-built tools, compiler feedback quality is a first-class design concern — choose languages where agents can self-correct from error output." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) — when a tool needs to parse multiple languages/frameworks, don't embed parsers. Instead, define a versioned JSON protocol over stdin/stdout and spawn parsers as subprocesses. Benefits: (1) core stays language-agnostic, (2) parsers can be written in whatever language parses that stack best, (3) fault isolation — crashing parser can't take down the host, (4) independent versioning, (5) community extensibility without forking. The protocol IS the interface. Trade-off: subprocess startup overhead (~100-500ms), but acceptable for scan-frequency operations." 2>&1 && \
PATTERN: Match failures as growth signals. When designing a lookup/match system, make the 'not found' path productive. Instead of returning empty, package the search context (what was queried, what related items exist) as a structured creation suggestion. The caller (agent or human) reviews and persists. This transforms every failure into a learning event. Applied in Abacus: Gherkin step with no matching Action → system returns related Routes/Entities/Pages + pre-filled Action template → agent creates Action → future queries succeed. The system gets smarter with each miss." 2>&1 && \
FACT: Go has a complete native ecosystem for building MCP-enabled CLI tools with graph storage: (1) github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0 — official MCP SDK with mcp.AddTool() and StdioTransport, (2) github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go — pattern matching with parameter extraction, (3) github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go/v38 — Gherkin file pa

content:
bd update aba-zca.16 -d "$(cat <<'DESC
## What

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON. This scanner captures the **primary backend route infrastructure** — Express is the actual server, not a legacy layer.

## Context

The hourly backend is an Express application. oRPC is used for contract definition and client SDK generation, but Express is the runtime route infrastructure. This means:

- **Express scanner** captures: actual route registration, middleware chains, auth guards, rate limiters, route mounting hierarchy, and routes that may not have oRPC contracts
- **oRPC scanner** captures: typed API contracts (input/output schemas, tags, summaries) used for client generation

Both scanners are essential and complementary. Together they give a complete picture:
- oRPC tells you WHAT the API contract looks like (types, schemas)
- Express tells you HOW the route is actually wired (middleware, guards, mounting)

The backend has 30+ route files in backend/src/routes/.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix composition)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);

// Middleware stacks (capture these — they reveal auth, validation, rate limiting)
router.post('/admin/users', requireAuth, requireRole('admin'), rateLimiter, createUser);
\`\`\`

### Middleware Extraction
Middleware names are valuable context for Actions �
bead:
bd update aba-zca.16 -d "$(cat <<'DESC'
## What

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON. This scanner captures the **primary backend route infrastructure** — Express is the actual server, not a legacy layer.

## Context

The hourly backend is an Express application. oRPC is used for contract definition and client SDK generation, but Express is the runtime route infrastructure. This means:

- **Express scanner** captures: actual route registration, middleware chains, auth guards, rate limiters, route mounting hierarchy, and routes that may not have oRPC contracts
- **oRPC scanner** captures: typed API contracts (input/output schemas, tags, summaries) used for client generation

Both scanners are essential and complementary. Together they give a complete picture:
- oRPC tells you WHAT the API contract looks like (types, schemas)
- Express tells you HOW the route is actually wired (middleware, guards, mounting)

The backend has 30+ route files in backend/src/routes/.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix composition)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);

// Middleware stacks (capture these — they reveal auth, validation, rate limiting)
router.post('/admin/users', requireAuth, requireRole('admin'), rateLimiter, createUser);
\`\`\`

### Middleware Extraction
Middleware names are valuable context for Actions — they reveal:
- Auth requirements (requireAuth, requireRole)
- Validation (validateBody, validateParams)
- Rate limiting (rateLimiter)
- File handling (upload.single)

Store as array in properties: \`"middleware": ["requireAuth", "requireRole", "validateBody"]\`

### Relationship to oRPC Scanner
When both scanners run, Abacus will have two Route nodes for the same endpoint — one from oRPC (contract) and one from Express (runtime). The CLI scan command should detect these overlaps and create \`relates_to\` edges between them. This is handled by the SyncScanResults logic in the Go core, not by this scanner.

## Testing

- [ ] Parses router.get/post/put/delete → correct Route nodes
- [ ] Handles parameterized routes (:id, :slug)
- [ ] Extracts middleware chain names
- [ ] Detects app.use() prefix mounting and composes full paths
- [ ] Handles router.route() chaining
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly Express route files

## Validation

- [ ] All 30+ hourly Express route files scanned
- [ ] Middleware chains captured (reveals auth/validation requirements)
- [ ] Route mounting prefixes correctly composed
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output

## Files

- scanners/express/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/express/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/express/__tests__/

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (Scanner Protocol v1 schema)
DESC
)" 2>&1 && \
aba-zca

content: Go module cache at /Users/mjn/go/pkg is read-only in the Claude Code sandbox. Must use GOMODCACHE=/tmp/.claude_scratch/gomodcache GONOSUMDB=* GONOSUMCHECK=* for all go commands.
Preserved existing .gitignore entries (.dolt/, *.db) from beads init and appended new Go-specific entries.
modernc.org/sqlite supports FTS5 and unixepoch() out of the box — no special build tags needed. GOMODCACHE must be an absolute path.
Used content-sync FTS5 (content='nodes', content_rowid='rowid') with triggers rather than external-content manual sync, keeping FTS automatically in sync with the nodes table.

Pure-Go SQLite setup with modernc.org/sqlite: driver name is 'sqlite' (NOT 'sqlite3' which is mattn's CGO driver). Pragmas must be individual Exec() calls, not batched. Set WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, foreign_keys=ON, cache_size=-64000 (64MB), busy_timeout=5000. If any pragma fails, Close() the db before returning to prevent leaked connections." 2>/dev/null

FTS5 content-sync trigger pattern for SQLite. The delete command syntax is counterintuitive: INSERT INTO table_fts(table_fts, rowid, cols) VALUES ('delete', old.rowid, old.cols). Table name appears as both target and column. UPDATE triggers must delete-then-insert (FTS5 has no native update). content_rowid='rowid' ties to SQLite's implicit rowid — breaks if table uses WITHOUT ROWID." 2>/dev/null

SQLite graph adjacency list with enforced types. CHECK constraints on kind columns enforce enums at DB level. UNIQUE(src_id, dst_id, kind) prevents duplicate edges of same kind but allows multiple edge types between same nodes. ON DELETE CASCADE on edges auto-cleans when nodes are deleted. Properties as JSON TEXT DEFAULT '{}' — extensible without migrations, queryable via json_extract(). Adding new kinds requires schema migration (intentional — schema is the contract)." 2>/dev/null

Cobra CLI with ldflags version injection. Declare 'var Version = \"dev\"' (must be var, not const — linker can't override constants). Build with '-ldflags \"-X full/package/path.Version=$(VERSION)\"'. Full import path required — wrong path silently does nothing. Cobra's rootCmd.Version field + SetVersionTemplate gives --version flag for free. VERSION ?= $(shell git describe --tags --always --dirty) with fallback to 'dev'." 2>/dev/null
Used a --dir flag on init command to allow testing in temp directories. This keeps tests isolated without needing to chdir.
cobra rootCmd is a package-level var, so tests reuse it across test functions. The --dir flag on init avoids test pollution by pointing at t.TempDir().

ValidateOutput returns []string (error messages) rather than error, making it composable — callers can check len(errs) == 0 and format/join as needed. The runner joins them with semicolons for its error return.

FTS5 content-sync join requires joining on rowid (n.rowid = fts.rowid), not on id. The content='nodes' and content_rowid='rowid' config in the FTS5 virtual table means FTS stores rowid references, not the text id primary key.
Used sql.NullString for nullable fields (source_file, scan_hash) to handle NULL values cleanly in scan operations, converting to *string pointers on the GraphNode struct.

Recursive CTE with UNION (not UNION ALL) for cycle-safe bidirectional graph traversal in SQLite. UNION inherently deduplicates visited nodes, preventing infinite loops in cyclic graphs without maintaining an explicit visited set. Query shape: WITH RECURSIVE connected(id, depth) AS (VALUES(?,0) UNION SELECT e.dst_id, c.depth+1 FROM edges e JOIN connected c ON e.src_id=c.id WHERE c.depth<? UNION SELECT e.src_id, c.depth+1 FROM edges e JOIN connected c ON e.dst_id=c.id WHERE c.depth<?). Two UNION branches traverse both edge directions." 2>/dev/null

validNodeKinds and validEdgeKinds are duplicated across db/types.go (Go constants) and scanner/validate.go (map[string]bool allowlists). Adding a new node or edge kind requires updating BOTH locations plus the SQLite CHECK constraint in schema.go. These are NOT derived from a single source of truth. This is the primary extensibility gotcha — forgetting one location causes silent validation failures." 2>/dev/null

RunAll merges scanner outputs by concatenation but does NOT run cross-scanner validation. Edges from scanner A referencing nodes from scanner B pass per-scanner validation but could fail at DB insert time. Also, two scanners emitting the same node ID results in last-write-wins via BulkUpsertNodes. No dedup detection exists at the merge layer. This is acceptable for MVP but needs SyncScanResults logic to handle properly." 2>/dev/null

Go repository testing with real SQLite via t.TempDir() — no mocking. setupTestDB creates a real in-memory or temp-file SQLite DB with full schema, registered for cleanup via t.Cleanup. This ensures DB behavior (triggers, constraints, FTS5) is tested faithfully. Combined with builder helpers (makeNode, validOutput) that provide default-valid test data where tests modify one field to trigger specific errors." 2>/dev/null

Subprocess plugin system with fail-open aggregation. RunAll captures per-scanner errors into MergedScanOutput.Errors and continues executing remaining scanners. Callers must check len(merged.Errors) > 0 after RunAll — a nil error return does NOT mean all scanners succeeded. This is the standard pattern for batch operations where partial results are more valuable than all-or-nothing." 2>/dev/null
